data.title: Noah Holdings reports Q2 results
data.description:
• Noah Holdings press release (NYSE:NOAH): Q2
  • Revenue of $87.9M.
  • As of June 30, total assets under management stood at RMB 145.1 billion (US$20.3 billion), with USD-denominated assets under management up 7.4% to US$5.8 billion and USD-denominated assets under advisory rising 6.6% to US$9.1 billion, underscoring the success of the Company's product depth and global allocation capabilities.

data.title: Spain's retail sales rise in July, inflation expected to stay steady
data.description:
• Retail sales in Spain increased 4.70% in July of 2025 over the same month in the previous year.

  • Retail sales in Spain decreased 0.40% in July.

  • Spain's annual inflation is expected at a five-month high of 2.7% in August 2025, matching July's reading and just below market forecasts of 2.8%.

  • ETFs: (NYSEARCA:EWP), (NASDAQ:EUFN)

data.title: France GDP expected to rise 0.8% Y/Y, inflation expected to lower
data.description:
France's GDP expanded 0.80% in Q2 2025 over the same quarter of the previous year, higher than estimates of 0.8%

GDP expanded 0.30% in Q2 over the previous quarter.

France’s annual inflation rate expected to ease to 0.9% in August, below market expectations of holding steady at July’s 1%.

CPI increased by 0.4% M/M in August 2025, picking up from a 0.2% gain in July but lower than the expected 0.5% growth.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:EWQ)

Currency: (EUR:USD)

data.title: Germany's retail sales rise 1.9% Y/Y in July, missing estimates

  • Currency: (EUR:USD)

data.title: Trump officials say end of low-value package tariff exemption is permanent - report
data.description:
The U.S. tariff exemption for package shipments valued under $800 ends permanently on Friday, with a six-month transition period under which postal service shippers can opt to pay a flat duty of $80 to $200 per package depending on the country of origin, reported Reuters.

The report, which cited Trump administration officials, said the U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP) agency will begin collecting normal duty rates on all global parcel imports, regardless of value, after 12:01 a.m. EDT (0401 GMT) on Friday.

"President Trump's ending of the deadly de minimis loophole will save thousands of American lives by restricting the flow of narcotics and other dangerous prohibited items, and add up to $10 billion a year in tariff revenues to our Treasury," White House trade adviser Peter Navarro told reporters, according to the report.

The move comes after President Trump in July signed an executive order that expanded the suspension of duty-free de minimis treatment for low-value shipments to a global scale, after suspending the provision for shipments from China and Hong Kong in May.

 The loophole was exploited by fast fashion retailers like SHEIN, as well as Temu (NASDAQ:PDD) to ship packages duty-free from China. As a result of the exemption, the volume of de minimis shipments entering the U.S. exploded from 134M to 1.36B over a 9-year period.

The Reuters report added that based on CBP guidance, parcels would be charged $80 from countries with Trump-imposed duty rates below 16%, such as Britain and the European Union, $160 from countries between 16% and 25%, such as Indonesia and Vietnam, and $200 from countries above 25%, including China, Brazil, India, and Canada.
